glossary.def: A model whose trained parameters are downloadable, so you can run it offline, fine-tune it, and pin a version forever. It says nothing about the training data or code, which are almost always withheld — Llama, Mistral, DeepSeek, and Qwen all ship weights without their corpora.

The stricter bar set by the Open Source Initiative's 2024 definition: weights plus enough data information and training code to reproduce the system, under a recognized open license. Most 'open' LLMs are open-weight rather than open-source; OLMo and Pythia are among the few that clear this bar.

A model reachable only as a hosted API — you send tokens in and get tokens out, billed per token, and never possess the weights. GPT, Claude, and Gemini flagships are closed; you rent frontier capability and managed operations in exchange for giving up possession, fine-tuning of the base, and version control.

A permissive open-source license (Apache-2.0, MIT) imposes minimal conditions and no field-of-use limits. A community or source-available license (such as Meta's Llama terms) is commercially usable but adds carve-outs — acceptable-use policies, naming requirements, or a scale clause — that bite a minority of users hard.

A clause, attached to nearly every modern release whether open or closed, that forbids specific harmful uses of the model. It is a real, enforceable restriction that travels with even 'open' weights, which is why open does not mean rule-free.

The monthly token volume n* = F / (p - m) at which renting an API (cost p per token) and self-hosting (fixed cost F plus marginal m per token) cost the same. Below it, renting is cheaper; above it, owning the weights wins and keeps winning as the fixed cost amortizes over more tokens.

A provision in Meta's Llama community license requiring a separate agreement from Meta if the licensee's products exceed 700 million monthly active users. It is the canonical example of a community-license carve-out that only affects hyperscaler-grade deployments but constrains them sharply.

Groeneveld, D., Beltagy, I., Walsh, P. et al. (2024). OLMo: Accelerating the Science of Language Models. arXiv preprint.
